03 Flexbox Mastery — One Axis, Total Control

← Back to lessons

Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout system. It distributes space along a single axis (row or column). Once you internalize the main-axis vs cross-axis model, every flex property snaps into place.

The Mental Model: A Tube of Items

Imagine a tube. Items flow through it in one direction. Flexbox gives you control over:

  1. Direction — which way the tube points (row or column)
  2. Alignment on the main axis — how items are distributed along the tube's length
  3. Alignment on the cross axis — how items are positioned perpendicular to the tube
  4. Space distribution — how leftover space is divided among items
  5. Wrapping — what happens when items overflow the tube
/* The main axis vs cross axis model */

flex-direction: row (default)
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  main axis →→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→→   │
│  ↕ cross axis                           │
│  [item 1] [item 2] [item 3] [item 4]   │
│                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

flex-direction: column
┌───────────────┐
│  main axis ↓  │  cross axis →→
│  [item 1]     │
│  [item 2]     │
│  [item 3]     │
│  [item 4]     │
└───────────────┘

Everything is relative to the main axis. justify-content always distributes along the main axis. align-items always aligns on the cross axis. When you change flex-direction from row to column, these properties don't change what they do — the axes swap under them.

Container Properties (Parent)

justify-content — main axis distribution

/* How items are distributed along the main axis */
.container { display: flex; }

justify-content: flex-start;     /* |||______ (default) */
justify-content: flex-end;       /* ______||| */
justify-content: center;         /* ___|||___ */
justify-content: space-between;  /* |___||___| */
justify-content: space-around;   /* _|__|__|_ */
justify-content: space-evenly;   /* __|__|__|__ */

/* Tailwind equivalents: */
/* justify-start, justify-end, justify-center,
   justify-between, justify-around, justify-evenly */

space-between vs space-around vs space-evenly. space-between puts space only BETWEEN items (first and last touch the edges). space-around puts equal space around each item (edges get half-space). space-evenly puts truly equal space everywhere (edges get the same space as between items). In practice, space-between is used 90% of the time.

align-items — cross axis alignment

/* How items are aligned on the cross axis */
align-items: stretch;      /* default — items stretch to fill cross axis */
align-items: flex-start;   /* items at the start of the cross axis */
align-items: flex-end;     /* items at the end of the cross axis */
align-items: center;       /* items centered on the cross axis */
align-items: baseline;     /* items aligned by their text baselines */

/* Tailwind: items-stretch, items-start, items-end,
   items-center, items-baseline */

The perfect centering pattern

/* Center anything, both axes, in one line */
.centered {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;  /* center on main axis */
  align-items: center;      /* center on cross axis */
}

/* Even shorter with place-content (grid shorthand that works in flex) */
.centered {
  display: grid;
  place-content: center;
}

/* Tailwind: flex items-center justify-center */

Before flexbox, vertical centering was a nightmare. It required hacks with absolute positioning, transforms, table-cell display, or negative margins. Now it's three properties. This alone justified flexbox's existence.

Item Properties (Children)

flex-grow, flex-shrink, flex-basis — the flex trinity

These three properties control how items share space. They're confusing until you understand the algorithm:

/* Step 1: Each item gets its flex-basis size (or content size) */
/* Step 2: Calculate leftover space (container - total bases) */
/* Step 3: Distribute leftover space proportionally by flex-grow */

.item { flex: 1; }  /* shorthand for: flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: 0 */

/* Three items all flex: 1 = each gets 1/3 of the space */
/* One item flex: 2, two items flex: 1 = 2/4, 1/4, 1/4 */
flex-grow distributes EXTRA space
/* Container: 600px
   Item A basis: 100px, grow: 1
   Item B basis: 100px, grow: 2

   Leftover: 600 - 200 = 400px
   A gets 400 * (1/3) = 133px → total 233px
   B gets 400 * (2/3) = 267px → total 367px */
flex-shrink removes EXCESS space
/* Container: 400px
   Item A basis: 300px, shrink: 1
   Item B basis: 300px, shrink: 2

   Excess: 600 - 400 = 200px to remove
   A loses 200 * (1/3) = 67px → total 233px
   B loses 200 * (2/3) = 133px → total 167px */

Tailwind connection. flex-1 is flex: 1 1 0% (grow equally, basis of 0). flex-auto is flex: 1 1 auto (grow equally, basis of content size). flex-none is flex: 0 0 auto (don't grow or shrink). These three cover 95% of use cases.

align-self — per-item cross axis override

/* Override the container's align-items for one child */
.container { display: flex; align-items: flex-start; }
.special-item { align-self: flex-end; }  /* just this item at the bottom */

/* Tailwind: self-start, self-end, self-center, self-stretch */

Wrapping: When Items Overflow

/* Default: items shrink to fit, never wrap */
.container { display: flex; flex-wrap: nowrap; }

/* Allow wrapping to new lines */
.container { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; }

/* With wrap, align-content controls spacing between LINES */
.container {
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  align-content: flex-start;  /* pack lines to the top */
  gap: 1rem;                  /* space between items */
}

/* Tailwind: flex-wrap, flex-nowrap, content-start, gap-4 */

gap is the modern way to space flex items. Before gap, you'd use margins and deal with the "extra margin on the last item" problem. gap only adds space BETWEEN items, never on the edges. It works in both flex and grid.

Common Flexbox Patterns

Pattern 1: Header with logo left, actions right

.header {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  align-items: center;
  padding: 1rem 2rem;
}
/* Tailwind: flex justify-between items-center px-8 py-4 */

Pattern 2: Push one item to the far end

.nav {
  display: flex;
  gap: 1rem;
}
.nav .logout {
  margin-left: auto;  /* pushes this item to the far right */
}
/* Tailwind: ml-auto on the item */

Pattern 3: Equal-width columns

.columns {
  display: flex;
  gap: 1rem;
}
.columns > * {
  flex: 1;  /* each child gets equal space */
}
/* Tailwind: flex gap-4, children get flex-1 */

Pattern 4: Sidebar + main content

.layout {
  display: flex;
}
.sidebar {
  flex: 0 0 280px;  /* fixed width, don't grow or shrink */
}
.main {
  flex: 1;          /* take all remaining space */
}
/* Tailwind: flex, sidebar w-[280px] flex-none, main flex-1 */

Pattern 5: Vertical centering with a fixed height

.hero {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  justify-content: center;
  align-items: center;
  min-height: 100vh;
}
/* Tailwind: flex flex-col justify-center items-center min-h-screen */

Check Your Understanding

1. In a flex-direction: column container, which axis does justify-content control?

The vertical axis. justify-content always controls the main axis. When direction is column, the main axis is vertical. So justify-content: center centers items vertically.

2. What does flex: 1 actually expand to?

flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: 0%. This means: take equal share of space, allow shrinking, start from 0 width (so all space is distributed by flex-grow ratios).

3. How do you push a single item to the far right in a flex row?

margin-left: auto on that item. Auto margins in flex absorb all available space in that direction. In Tailwind: ml-auto.

4. What's the difference between align-items and align-content?

align-items aligns individual items on the cross axis within their line. align-content distributes space between multiple lines (only matters with flex-wrap: wrap).

5. Why is gap better than margins for spacing flex items?

gap only adds space BETWEEN items, not on the outer edges. With margins, you'd need to remove the margin from the first/last item. gap is cleaner and works the same in both flex and grid.

Exercise: Build These Layouts with Flexbox

Using only flexbox (no grid), build these common UI patterns in your editor:

  1. Navigation bar: Logo on the left, links in the center, sign-in button on the right
  2. Card row: Three equal-width cards with a gap, where the middle card is taller
  3. Holy grail footer: Three columns (about, links, social) that stack vertically on mobile
  4. Chat bubble: Avatar on the left (fixed size), message on the right (flexible width)

For each, write the CSS first, then the equivalent Tailwind classes. Compare.

Recap

  1. Flexbox is one-dimensional — main axis + cross axis
  2. justify-content = main axis, align-items = cross axis
  3. Changing flex-direction swaps the axes under the properties
  4. flex-grow distributes extra space, flex-shrink handles overflow
  5. flex: 1 = equal sharing, flex: none = fixed size
  6. margin-left: auto pushes items right (or margin-top: auto pushes down)
  7. gap > margins for spacing between items
  8. flex-wrap + align-content for multi-line layouts
← Lesson 02: Layout Lesson 04: CSS Grid →
